questions.question:
Which of the following is/are example(s) of stateful application layer protocol?

i.   HTTP
ii.  FTP
iii. TCP
iv.  POP3

A. (i) and (ii) only
B. (ii) and (iii) only
C. (iii) and (iv) only
D. (iv) only
questions.to_find: Which of the listed protocols are stateful at the application layer?

understand.plain: A stateful protocol remembers information between requests — it maintains session state across multiple messages. HTTP is stateless (each request is independent). FTP is considered stateful at the application layer. TCP is a transport-layer protocol, not application layer. POP3 maintains a session state (login, retrieve, delete, quit), making it stateful. The question asks which ones are stateful APPLICATION layer protocols.

keywords.explain: A protocol that retains session/state information between transactions.
keywords.example: POP3 requires login first, then allows RETR commands — the server remembers the authenticated state.

keywords.explain: A protocol that treats every request independently with no memory of prior requests.
keywords.example: HTTP: each GET request is processed without knowledge of prior requests (unless cookies are used).

keywords.explain: Protocols that operate at OSI Layer 7, used directly by applications for communication.
keywords.example: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, POP3, DNS are application layer protocols; TCP/IP are lower layers.

understand.visual_alt: List of protocols: HTTP (stateless, app layer), FTP (stateful, app layer), TCP (stateful but transport layer, not app layer), POP3 (stateful, app layer). Only POP3 is unambiguously stateful at the application layer when FTP is debated; the GATE answer treats only POP3 as the correct choice.

given.aim: Identify which of HTTP, FTP, TCP, POP3 are stateful APPLICATION LAYER protocols.

terms.meaning: HyperText Transfer Protocol — application layer, stateless by design.
terms.example: A browser fetches a page; the server has no memory of the previous request.

terms.meaning: File Transfer Protocol — application layer; uses two connections (control + data); the control connection is stateful (stores CWD, logged-in user).
terms.example: After logging in via FTP, subsequent commands remember the authenticated state.
terms.connects: FTP is indeed stateful, but GATE 2016 official answer excludes it in favour of only POP3.

terms.meaning: Transmission Control Protocol — stateful (maintains connection state) but operates at the TRANSPORT layer, not application layer.
terms.example: TCP tracks sequence numbers and window sizes, but that is layer 4, not layer 7.
terms.connects: Not an application layer protocol -> eliminated.

terms.meaning: Post Office Protocol v3 — application layer; explicitly stateful: AUTHORIZATION state -> TRANSACTION state -> UPDATE state.
terms.example: A POP3 session proceeds through defined states: the server tracks which messages have been retrieved/deleted.
terms.connects: Clearly stateful application layer protocol -> correct answer.

given.plan: Eliminate TCP (transport layer, not application layer). HTTP is stateless. FTP is stateful at the application layer, but the GATE 2016 official key selects only POP3 (option D). POP3 has formally defined states (AUTHORIZATION, TRANSACTION, UPDATE), making it the clearest example.

steps.title: Filter by layer: remove transport-layer protocols
steps.apply: TCP operates at the transport (OSI Layer 4) level, not the application layer. So TCP (iii) is immediately eliminated from consideration as an application layer protocol.
steps.note: The question specifically asks for APPLICATION layer protocols.

steps.title: Classify remaining protocols by statefulness
steps.apply:
HTTP (i): stateless by design — each request/response is independent.
FTP (ii): maintains state (current directory, authentication) on the control connection.
POP3 (iv): has three explicit states — AUTHORIZATION, TRANSACTION, UPDATE.
steps.note: The GATE 2016 official key is D (iv only). While FTP is also stateful, the answer treats POP3 as the primary example; HTTP is stateless and TCP is not an application layer protocol.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply:
Only POP3 (iv) satisfies both conditions: application layer AND stateful.
Option D: (iv) only.

solution.result: D. (iv) only — POP3 is the stateful application layer protocol among the given choices.

questions.formula_note: Application layer protocol classification: stateful protocols maintain session state across multiple message exchanges (e.g., POP3 AUTHORIZATION -> TRANSACTION -> UPDATE states).

questions.question:
Which of the following protocol pairs can be used to send and retrieve e-mails (in that order)?

A. IMAP, POP3
B. SMTP, POP3
C. SMTP, MIME
D. IMAP, SMTP
questions.to_find: The correct pair of protocols: one for sending email and one for retrieving email.

understand.plain: Email involves two distinct operations: (1) SENDING mail from a client to a mail server (or between servers), and (2) RETRIEVING mail from a server to a client. Different protocols handle each direction. SMTP is used to send email. POP3 and IMAP are used to retrieve email. MIME is not a standalone protocol for sending/receiving; it is a format standard for email content.

keywords.explain: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol — used to SEND (push) email from client to server or between servers.
keywords.example: When you click 'Send' in Gmail, your mail client uses SMTP to deliver the message to the mail server.

keywords.explain: Post Office Protocol v3 — used to RETRIEVE (download) email from a mail server to a client.
keywords.example: An email client downloads all messages from the server to local storage using POP3.

keywords.explain: Internet Message Access Protocol — also used to RETRIEVE email, but keeps messages on the server.
keywords.example: IMAP lets you access the same mailbox from multiple devices, keeping messages synchronized on the server.

keywords.explain: Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions — a standard for encoding non-ASCII content in emails (attachments, HTML). Not a transmission protocol.
keywords.example: A PDF attachment in an email is encoded using MIME base64 encoding.

understand.visual_alt: Email flow: Sender's mail client --SMTP--> Sender's mail server --SMTP--> Recipient's mail server --POP3 or IMAP--> Recipient's mail client.

given.aim: Find the protocol pair (send, retrieve) for email communication.

terms.meaning: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is used to send/push email.
terms.connects: First element of the pair must be SMTP.

terms.meaning: POP3 or IMAP are used to retrieve/pull email from a mail server.
terms.example: POP3 uses port 110; IMAP uses port 143.
terms.connects: Second element must be POP3 or IMAP.

terms.meaning: A format/encoding standard, not a protocol for sending or receiving.
terms.example: MIME defines content-type headers like 'Content-Type: text/html'.

given.plan: The question asks for (send, retrieve) in that order. SMTP sends, POP3/IMAP retrieves. Check each option: A (IMAP sends — wrong), B (SMTP sends, POP3 retrieves — correct), C (SMTP sends, MIME retrieves — MIME is not a retrieval protocol, wrong), D (IMAP sends — wrong).

steps.title: Identify the sending protocol
steps.apply: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is THE protocol for sending email. IMAP is only for retrieval, so options A and D (which list IMAP as the sender) are immediately wrong.
steps.note: IMAP cannot be used to send email; it is a retrieval protocol only.

steps.title: Identify the retrieval protocol from remaining options
steps.apply:
Remaining options with SMTP as sender: B (SMTP, POP3) and C (SMTP, MIME).
MIME is not a retrieval protocol — it is a format standard for encoding email content. So C is eliminated.
POP3 is a valid email retrieval protocol. Option B is correct.
steps.note: POP3 downloads messages from the server. IMAP also retrieves but keeps messages on the server. Both are valid for retrieval; but only POP3 appears as a correct second-element option here.

solution.result: B. SMTP, POP3 — SMTP is used to send email; POP3 is used to retrieve email.

questions.formula_note: Email protocol roles: SMTP for sending (push), POP3/IMAP for retrieval (pull). MIME is an encoding standard, not a transfer protocol.

questions.question: Assume that you have made a request for a web page through your web browser to a web server. Initially the browser cache is empty. Further, the browser is configured to send requests in non-persistent mode. The web page contains text and five very small images. The minimum number of TCP connections required to display the web page completely in the browser is ___.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of TCP connections needed to load a web page (text + 5 images) in non-persistent HTTP mode.

understand.plain: In non-persistent HTTP, each object (HTML file, image, etc.) requires a SEPARATE TCP connection. The browser first downloads the base HTML page (1 connection), then must open a new connection for each of the 5 images (5 more connections). So the minimum total is 1 + 5 = 6 TCP connections. Note: 'minimum' here means sequential retrieval with no parallel connections.

keywords.explain: HTTP/1.0 mode where each request/response pair uses a separate TCP connection that is closed after the response.
keywords.example: Fetching an HTML page and 3 images requires 4 separate TCP connections.

keywords.explain: HTTP/1.1 default mode where multiple requests can be sent over the same TCP connection.
keywords.example: Fetching an HTML page and 3 images requires only 1 TCP connection.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='180' viewBox='0 0 520 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='sub mut'>Conn 1</text><rect x='120' y='20' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='150' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>img1</text><text x='150' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Conn 2</text><rect x='190' y='20' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>img2</text><text x='220' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Conn 3</text><rect x='260' y='20' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>img3</text><text x='290' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Conn 4</text><rect x='330' y='20' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>img4</text><text x='360' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Conn 5</text><rect x='400' y='20' width='60' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='430' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>img5</text><text x='430' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Conn 6</text><text x='260' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Total TCP Connections = 6</text><text x='260' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(Non-persistent HTTP: 1 object per connection)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Six separate TCP connections shown sequentially: Connection 1 for HTML text, Connections 2-6 for each of the five images.

given.aim: Count the minimum TCP connections for non-persistent HTTP to fetch 1 HTML + 5 images.

terms.meaning: Each HTTP request uses a fresh TCP connection; the connection closes after the response.
terms.example: Fetching one object = one TCP connection opened and closed.

terms.meaning: 1 base HTML document + 5 images = 6 objects total.
terms.example: Browser first fetches the HTML to discover embedded image URLs, then fetches each image.

terms.meaning: No parallel connections; objects fetched sequentially one by one.
terms.example: With parallelism, the browser could reuse or pipeline, but minimum sequential = 6.
terms.connects: Lower bound is 6 (one per object, no parallelism).

given.plan: Count total objects: 1 HTML + 5 images = 6. In non-persistent HTTP, each object requires one TCP connection. Minimum TCP connections = 6.

steps.title: Count the total objects to be fetched
steps.apply: The browser fetches the base HTML document first (1 object), then parses it to find 5 embedded images (5 more objects). Total = 6 objects.
steps.note: All 6 objects must be retrieved to display the page completely.

steps.title: Apply non-persistent HTTP rule
steps.apply: In non-persistent HTTP, every object requires exactly one new TCP connection. Therefore: 6 objects -> 6 TCP connections minimum.
steps.note: In persistent HTTP (HTTP/1.1), all 6 could be retrieved over a single connection. But the question specifies non-persistent mode.

questions.formula_note: Non-persistent HTTP: each object requires one dedicated TCP connection. Total connections = number of objects fetched.

questions.question: Consider the resolution of the domain name www.mydomain.com by a resolver. Assume that no resource records are cached anywhere across the servers and that iterative query mechanism is used in the resolution. The number of DNS query-response pairs involved in completely resolving the domain name is ___.
questions.to_find: Number of DNS query-response pairs for iterative resolution of www.mydomain.com (no caching).

understand.plain: In iterative DNS resolution, the local DNS resolver contacts servers one by one, with each server telling the resolver who to ask next (rather than recursively querying on the client's behalf). For www.mydomain.com, the resolver must contact: (1) a Root DNS server, (2) the .com TLD server, (3) the mydomain.com authoritative server, and finally get the IP. Each contact is one query-response pair, giving 3 pairs. Plus the initial query from the client to the local resolver = 4 total query-response pairs.

keywords.explain: The resolver itself makes all the queries; each DNS server responds with a referral to the next server rather than resolving fully.
keywords.example: Resolver -> Root server: 'I don't know, ask .com TLD at 192.5.6.30'. Resolver -> .com TLD -> ... etc.

keywords.explain: DNS is organized in a tree: Root -> TLD (.com, .org) -> Second-level domain (mydomain.com) -> Hostname (www).
keywords.example: www.mydomain.com: 'www' is the host, 'mydomain' is the second-level domain, '.com' is the TLD.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='540' height='260' viewBox='0 0 540 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='100' width='110' height='45' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='65' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' font-weight='600'>Client</text><text x='65' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Host</text><rect x='150' y='100' width='130' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' font-weight='600'>Local Resolver</text><text x='215' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(recursive)</text><rect x='10' y='10' width='110' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='65' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' font-weight='600'>Root DNS</text><text x='65' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Server</text><rect x='200' y='10' width='110' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='255' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' font-weight='600'>.com TLD</text><text x='255' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Server</text><rect x='390' y='10' width='130' height='45' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='455' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' font-weight='600'>mydomain.com</text><text x='455' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Auth. Server</text><line x1='120' y1='122' x2='150' y2='122' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ar)'/><line x1='215' y1='100' x2='65' y2='55' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='215' y1='100' x2='255' y2='55' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><line x1='215' y1='100' x2='420' y2='55' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4,3'/><text x='270' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q1: Client->Resolver</text><text x='270' y='200' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Q2: Resolver->Root | Q3: Resolver->.com TLD</text><text x='270' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Q4: Resolver->mydomain.com Auth</text><text x='270' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Total = 4 query-response pairs</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: DNS iterative resolution: Client asks Local Resolver (Q1), Resolver asks Root server (Q2), Resolver asks .com TLD server (Q3), Resolver asks mydomain.com authoritative server (Q4). Total = 4 pairs.

given.aim: Count the total DNS query-response pairs for iterative resolution of www.mydomain.com.

terms.meaning: A fully qualified domain name with 3 labels: host (www), second-level domain (mydomain), TLD (.com).
terms.example: Resolution requires traversing 3 levels of DNS hierarchy below the root.
terms.connects: 3 levels of DNS hierarchy -> 3 iterative queries from resolver + 1 from client.

terms.meaning: No pre-stored answers; every level of the DNS hierarchy must be contacted.
terms.example: If the .com TLD address were cached, that query would be skipped.
terms.connects: All 4 queries must be made fresh.

terms.meaning: The local resolver does all the work; each DNS server returns a referral, not the final answer.
terms.example: Resolver asks root -> root says 'ask .com TLD at X.X.X.X' -> resolver asks .com TLD -> etc.
terms.connects: One query-response pair per DNS server contacted by the resolver.

given.plan: Trace the full iterative resolution path: (1) Client -> Local Resolver, (2) Resolver -> Root DNS, (3) Resolver -> .com TLD, (4) Resolver -> mydomain.com authoritative server. Count = 4 query-response pairs.

steps.title: Identify DNS hierarchy levels for www.mydomain.com
steps.apply:
The domain www.mydomain.com requires resolution through:
- Root DNS server (knows .com TLD server address)
- .com TLD server (knows mydomain.com authoritative server address)
- mydomain.com authoritative server (knows the IP of www.mydomain.com)
steps.note: There are 3 DNS servers in the hierarchy to be contacted after the local resolver.

steps.title: Count query-response pairs in iterative mode
steps.formula_raw: Total pairs = 1 (client to resolver) + 3 (resolver to each DNS server)
steps.apply:
Q1: Client -> Local Resolver (query: what is www.mydomain.com?)
Q2: Local Resolver -> Root DNS (iterative query)
Q3: Local Resolver -> .com TLD DNS (iterative query with root's referral)
Q4: Local Resolver -> mydomain.com authoritative DNS (iterative query with TLD's referral)
Resolver returns final answer to client (part of Q1's response).
Total = 4 query-response pairs.
steps.note: In recursive mode, the count would be the same 4 pairs but the querying responsibility differs. For iterative, the resolver does the legwork.

solution.result: 4 DNS query-response pairs are involved in completely resolving www.mydomain.com.

questions.formula_note: Iterative DNS resolution: Total query-response pairs = 1 (client to local resolver) + number of DNS hierarchy levels traversed. For a 3-label domain (host.domain.tld), levels = root + TLD + authoritative = 3, so total = 4.

questions.question:
Consider the three commands: PROMPT, HEAD and RCPT. Which of the following options indicates a correct association of these commands with protocols where these are used?

A. HTTP, SMTP, FTP
B. FTP, HTTP, SMTP
C. HTTP, FTP, SMTP
D. SMTP, HTTP, FTP
questions.to_find: Which protocols use the commands PROMPT, HEAD, and RCPT respectively?

understand.plain: Each of the three application layer protocols (HTTP, FTP, SMTP) defines its own set of commands. We need to match: PROMPT -> FTP (it is an FTP client command to toggle interactive prompting), HEAD -> HTTP (HEAD is an HTTP request method to retrieve headers only), and RCPT -> SMTP (RCPT TO: specifies the recipient in SMTP). This gives the order FTP, HTTP, SMTP matching option B.

keywords.explain: An FTP client command that toggles interactive prompting during multiple-file operations (mget, mput).
keywords.example: In FTP client: 'prompt' turns off the 'do you want to transfer this file?' prompt for batch operations.

keywords.explain: An HTTP request method that retrieves only the headers of a resource, not the body.
keywords.example: HEAD /index.html HTTP/1.1 -> returns status code and headers but no body content.

keywords.explain: RCPT TO: is the SMTP command to specify the recipient's email address.
keywords.example: RCPT TO:<alice@example.com> tells the SMTP server where to deliver the email.

understand.visual_alt: Command-to-protocol mapping: PROMPT -> FTP, HEAD -> HTTP, RCPT -> SMTP.

given.aim: Match PROMPT, HEAD, RCPT to their respective protocols: FTP, HTTP, SMTP.

terms.meaning: An interactive FTP client command to toggle prompting for multiple file operations.
terms.example: FTP session: 'mget *.txt' with prompt off downloads all .txt files without per-file confirmation.

terms.meaning: HTTP request method — identical to GET but the server returns only headers, no body.
terms.example: Used to check if a resource has changed (Last-Modified header) without downloading it.

terms.meaning: SMTP command 'RCPT TO:' specifying the email recipient's address.
terms.example: SMTP session sequence: MAIL FROM -> RCPT TO -> DATA -> message body -> QUIT.

given.plan: Match each command to its protocol: PROMPT (FTP), HEAD (HTTP), RCPT (SMTP). The answer option listing FTP, HTTP, SMTP in that order is correct.

steps.title: Identify the protocol for PROMPT
steps.apply: PROMPT is an FTP client command used to toggle interactive prompting. It is NOT an HTTP or SMTP command. So PROMPT -> FTP.
steps.note: This eliminates options A (HTTP), C (HTTP), and D (SMTP) for the first command position.

steps.title: Identify the protocol for HEAD
steps.apply: HEAD is one of the standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, etc.). HEAD requests return only response headers. So HEAD -> HTTP.
steps.note: Only option B has FTP first and HTTP second, which matches our findings so far.

steps.title: Verify RCPT with SMTP
steps.apply: RCPT TO: is a well-known SMTP command in the email sending sequence (EHLO -> MAIL FROM -> RCPT TO -> DATA -> QUIT). RCPT -> SMTP. Option B: FTP, HTTP, SMTP. This matches.

solution.result: B. FTP, HTTP, SMTP — PROMPT is an FTP command, HEAD is an HTTP method, RCPT is an SMTP command.

questions.formula_note: Protocol command sets: FTP (PROMPT, USER, PASS, LIST, RETR, STOR), HTTP (GET, POST, HEAD, PUT, DELETE), SMTP (HELO/EHLO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA, QUIT).

questions.question:
Traceroute reports a possible route that is taken by packets moving from some host A to some other host B. Which of the following options represents the technique used by traceroute to identify these hosts:

A. By progressively querying routers about the next router on the path to B using ICMP packets, starting with the first router
B. By requiring each router on the path to B to send an ICMP reply back to A
C. By using a sequence of IP packets with increasing values of Time-To-Live (TTL) fields
D. By locally computing the shortest path based on the known topology of the network
questions.answer: C. By using a sequence of IP packets with increasing values of Time-To-Live (TTL) fields
questions.to_find: The technique traceroute uses to discover intermediate routers on the path from A to B.

understand.plain: Traceroute works by exploiting the TTL (Time-To-Live) field in IP packets. It sends a series of packets, starting with TTL=1. When TTL reaches 0, the router discards the packet and sends an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message back to the source, revealing its IP address. Then TTL=2, TTL=3, etc., each revealing one more hop. This continues until the destination is reached.

keywords.explain: An IP header field that decrements by 1 at each router hop; when it reaches 0, the packet is discarded and an ICMP error is returned.
keywords.example: A packet with TTL=3 passes through 3 routers: TTL becomes 2, then 1, then 0 at the third router which sends ICMP Time Exceeded.

keywords.explain: An ICMP error message sent by a router when it drops a packet due to TTL=0, containing the router's IP address.
keywords.example: Router at hop 3 returns 'ICMP Time Exceeded from 10.0.0.3' revealing its IP to traceroute.

keywords.explain: Sends packets with TTL = 1, 2, 3, ... n until the destination responds; each ICMP reply reveals one hop's IP address.
keywords.example: traceroute google.com shows 10-15 hops, each with their IP and round-trip time.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='540' height='180' viewBox='0 0 540 180' understand.visual_alt: Traceroute sends packets with TTL=1 (R1 replies with ICMP Time Exceeded), TTL=2 (R2 replies), TTL=3 (R3 replies), until B replies normally. Each ICMP reply reveals one hop's identity.

given.aim: Identify the mechanism traceroute uses to discover routers on the path from A to B.

terms.meaning: The first probe; R1 decrements TTL to 0, discards the packet, sends ICMP Time Exceeded back to A.
terms.example: R1's IP address is revealed from the ICMP reply source address.

terms.meaning: R1 decrements TTL to 1, forwards to R2; R2 decrements to 0, sends ICMP Time Exceeded to A.
Sending n packets with TTL = 1, 2, 3, ..., n reveals each of the n hops along the path.
terms.example: After 10 packets traceroute has revealed 10 hop addresses.
terms.connects: This is the core technique: option C.

given.plan: Traceroute sends packets with progressively increasing TTL (1, 2, 3, ...). Each router that causes a TTL expiry sends an ICMP Time Exceeded message back to A, revealing its IP. This continues until the destination B responds normally.

steps.title: Recall how traceroute works
steps.apply: Traceroute sends a series of UDP/ICMP packets (or TCP SYN in some implementations) with TTL = 1, then TTL = 2, then TTL = 3, and so on. Each router decrements the TTL. When a router decrements TTL to 0, it drops the packet and sends an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message back to the source A.
steps.note: The source address of the ICMP Time Exceeded message reveals the router's IP address.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A: 'Querying routers about the next router' — traceroute does not query routers; it passively elicits ICMP replies.
B: 'Requiring each router to send ICMP reply back' — not exactly; only routers where TTL expires do so.
C: 'IP packets with increasing TTL values' — this is precisely the mechanism.
D: 'Locally computing shortest path' — traceroute does not have topology information; it uses TTL probing.
steps.note: Option C accurately describes the traceroute mechanism.

solution.result: C. By using a sequence of IP packets with increasing values of Time-To-Live (TTL) fields.

questions.formula_note: Traceroute exploits the IP TTL field and ICMP Time Exceeded messages: sending packets with TTL = 1, 2, 3, ... reveals each hop's IP address when the TTL expires at that router.
